item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's the lack of coffee, but I just can't dig my head into this one. Minor timeline overlap that manifests as walking bodies. Yes, the fridge horror is there, but it all seems so… dulled.

Also, as mentioned above, the footnote linkage is broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gonna want to catch the formatting on the footnote in paragraph three, line three of the description. Isn't a footnote, just superscript.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man you just gave me so many ideas.

+1 enthusiastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It didn't happen in every timeline simultaneously, and there was a lag of a couple days between when whatever it was started in every other timeline and when the alt-Foundations were sufficiently disabled that they could no longer tag instances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I get the concept, but there's something I'm still not quite getting - the alarmingly high number of tagged people + no longer ambulatory corpses makes for an implication that something Very Bad just happened in the other timelines, sure. But… if we're talking extinction level events in every other timeline… who tagged the 41 million people?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am a huge fan of this SCP; I lean massively to the side of preferring apparently undramatic objects or phenomena which are mostly horrifying in their implications, and I love that the horror in this SCP is not that it's happening, but that it's no longer happening.

I like the sobriety of this SCP a lot and I don't personally think that it's overly hidden in meaning (that may be the result of edits, though.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(1) Exactly. This implies that something is going wrong simultaneously in enormous numbers of different timelines, but we see nothing.

(2) The epidemic of radiation poisoning is a side-effect of the tags. Whatever's happening, a significant percentage of the human population was tagged as being anomalous in at least one timeline. As the chance of SCP-2087 happening //at all/ is vanishingly tiny, this means that they have died at the exact same moment in almost every possible world.

(The "nonviable branching in nanoseconds" language in the addendum makes it even worse: not only are all of those other universes dead, something is still killing all but one branch from the current timeline.)

(3) SCP-2087-01 can't be contained from the timeline where it's active. It can only be contained from the timeline where the corresponding SCP-2087-02 is still alive. The "self-containment" of SCP-2087-01 is actually just the Foundation in some other timeline moving the living SCP-2087-02 to a containment facility which exists in two timelines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the necro, but I just wanted to see if I'm reading something correctly that I haven't seen spelled out in the comments yet.

Any given instance of the foundation would only be able to tag so many people in such a short period of time. Thus, the implication is that there are many millions of alternate timelines that suffered massive (or total?) casualties, all at once.

Hmmm, and as I write that, it occurs to me —

As for the sudden epidemic of radiation poisoning: gamma ray burst?

And if the earth got hit by a gamma ray burst in so many alternate timelines at the same time, we have no way of knowing if it doesn't occur in our timeline — or if it just took a little longer, and the sensitization of the planet is imminent? And since a gamma ray burst travels at the speed of light, we wouldn't know for certain until it hits us.

OK, I was 'meh' on this at first. But it's growing on me as the fridge horror kicks in.

(Also, I don't get why the containment procedures say the instances of -01 and -02 would "self-contain".)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to actually write The Maw, The Voice, The Pinhole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really is pretty brilliant.
Hory shet

item.title: Re: @Ophite re: SCP-2087
item.content:encoded:
Right. It's a little worse than that, though: all of the sudden, enough people in multiple alternate timelines died that 41m people ended up tagged in a very short time frame. Then all of our "dead here; living there" instances suddenly fell over and stopped being undead. Then we had a burst of ambient radiation so awful that we couldn't tell how many people just died.

When we finally started sampling again, we realized that 21% of the human population had, at some point, been tagged as anomalous by an alternate timeline, and many of them were tagged as anomalous in multiple timelines. Now, when we attempt to ping alternate timelines, we no longer get a response — at least on a nanosecond scale, any deviation from the timeline branch we are presently in appears to result in the death of the human performing the experiment. (And presumably all other humans.)

The last hope we have is that divergence is still possible on a Planck scale (i.e., the smallest possible unit of time), but we're unable to confirm even that.

item.title: Re: @Ophite re: SCP-2087
item.content:encoded:
I strongly agree with your recommended change; I had the same problem with my understanding and had to come to the comments to see the significance of the start of the last incident report.

To your question
The tags exploit the same property that makes the corpses move.

An SCP-2087-1 instance is "dead here, alive there"- the corpse in "our timeline" (let's call it Alpha) is moving because that person has some connection between their Alpha iteration and the ones in other timelines- Beta, Gamma, etc. The Beta or Gamma or Delta version of that person isn't dead, and is still moving, and that's the movement we see in the Alpha version.

The tag applied to an Alpha corpse isn't to identify the Alpha version of the person. The Alpha Foundation already knows that person is anomalous, because they're a moving corpse. The tag is intended to be visible across timelines- it originates in Alpha but is visible in the Beta, Gamma, etc. versions of that person, the same way that the movement originates in one of those versions but is visible in Alpha.

When the Alpha Foundation tags a corpse, the Beta Foundation (or the Foundation of whatever reality where that corpse's counterpart is still alive) starts seeing a tag in a living human, and knows that that person is linked to their counterparts.

The reverse also applies- if the Beta Foundation tags a corpse, the Alpha Foundation can now identify someone who is connected to their alternate counterparts, even though the Alpha version of that person isn't dead yet. From the Alpha timeline's perspective, that person has been "tagged in advance," as you say, and is now classified SCP-2087-2: "living here, dead there."

The "dead there" part of that classification is what makes the last incident report so worrying. We don't know exactly why 41 million people suddenly fit that category, but the fact that it happened implies a whole lot of people in other realities dying in a very short time, i.e., some catastrophic event affecting at least one alternate timeline, maybe more.

And since many people go their whole lives without showing any connection between their alternate selves, 41 million identified as "dead there" may only be a fraction of the number that actually died. If everyone in a given timeline died, but only 0.1% of those corpses kept moving, then only 0.1% of the corpses would be tagged- and we'd only see 1 new tag for every thousand alternate casualties.

The last collapsible points out that the geographic distribution of new tags matches the overall human population distribution, which means that this spread of tags is a lot like what we would see in that scenario- for every X people in a given area, there are Y new tags, where X and Y are constant across the planet.

(Sorry to necro, but Eta's suggestion is important to me.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am similarly confused, even after reading the attempted explanation on the previous page of this thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I 100% get this, can someone tell me if I've got it right?

People die in one timeline, but not others. The version of them that didn't die sometimes takes on the traits of their dead counterparts, but continues to function, i.e. someone whose head was cut off in another timeline is walking around without a head. Some people die of non-observable causes (internal injuries, illness, etc.) and the Foundation needs special methods to identify them. One day they find that 41 million people are suddenly affected, meaning something bad has happened in another time line.

There's a few holes in my understanding: do affected people eventually expire? And why is it that a mere 41 million deaths indicates the end of life in alternate timelines?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ophite, I love you but this concept is extremely similar to the premise of an episode of "Fringe" that aired back in 2010:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacksonville_%28Fringe%29

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haven't finished BioShock Infinite yet but this reminds me a lot of the Fringe episodes "Welcome to Westfield" (S4:E12) and "The Consultant" (S4:E18). The former episode involves people from alternate realities fusing together such that some of them had multiple pupils per eye and twice as many teeth, and they started to progressively get crazy. And the latter episode had the two universes so entangled that one person dying in a car crash caused their alternate self to die gruesomely seemingly out of nowhere.

Nice article, Ophite!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i feel like this is trying to do something but it cant fully get there. I understand where the horror should be but i don't think there's enough of it.

item.title: @Ophite re: SCP-2087
item.content:encoded:
Aha, another Ophite SCP! YAY!!!!

Okay. Sorry about the necropost, but I do have a few things to say, that will hopefully be interesting (yeah, I'm a dreamer!).

+ show block
– hide block

1) In order to make the SCP clearer, there is a very simple clarification to make, IMO.
Instead of

Foundation distributed radiation detectors returned approximately 41m positive Strontium-90 signals

Why not write
Foundation distributed radiation detectors returned approximately 41 million positive Strontium-90 signals
… because I simply didn't understand this little m after the 41 (I actually thought it was some kind of measurement for radioactivity), and it's the key to the whole story!

But, I have another question:
If individuals who somehow mess up the alternate lines ares only detectable when one of their alter egos dies, how can they be tagged in advance?
Not all people who die mess up with timelines. Not all people who mess up with timelines die in horrific accidents. So I don't understand how this is the chosen 'containment procedure', and how suddenly 41 million people appear contaminated. There is something not logical here - or am I missing the point entirely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This went from cute and a bit quirky (walking legs rejoining with their body?) to disturbing REAL quick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that this is essentially people with Tear Sickness from Bioshock Infinite bugs me to no end.

For those of you who don't know, Tear Sickness (referred to on the Bioshock Wiki as being "Merged") is what happens to some corpses in Infinite when Elizabeth enters a new universe/timeline via a tear. Living individuals often get nosebleeds as their memories try to compensate for the incongruity with the one they originated from. Dead individuals, however… well, it's somewhat inconsistent, but usually, if their corpse is in close proximity to an opened tear, they start phasing in and out of existence, have jerky movements, and talk in a distorted voice.

This, combined with the ability to move between alternate timelines the Foundation has, makes me see this as Bioshock Infinite: The SCP. And as much as I love Infinite, this concept doesn't feel original at all.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it. Tone is spot on as always and I'm glad there's no exposition in a scientific document.

Narratives are for tales. This is what a Foundation document should look like.

item.title: Re: Proposed Rewrite --
item.content:encoded: It would work. But I really like this as it is, because the horror is extremely subtle. The rewrite would make it too obvious as to why this is terribad.

item.title: Proposed Rewrite --
item.content:encoded:
The more I look at this SCP, the more unsatisfied I am with how it turned out. But since it's up here, I think I'll take an opportunity to see if the issues I need to fix aren't more structural than conceptual. The rewrite would look like this:

Containment stays basically the same. However, we contain everyone in a facility built with far more cells than people, because it's meant to contain the equivalent SCPs in other universes.

Transboundary contact is limited as a concept. Instead, what the Foundation does is to precommit to containing persons whose counterparts are radioactively tagged.

Description contains the two people we have right now, and includes their physical descriptions.

Addenda include:

A case report. (The hospital one.)

A brief, somewhat personal memo from a scientist discussing the theory here, and speculating about the dangers of contact with parallel timelines

A slightly different shutdown memo discussing the destruction of every other viable timeline.

What do people think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is absolutely hilarious. I had a different experience of the article (I liked it quite a bit) but that is nevertheless hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the same reason that an AU is the distance from the Earth to the Sun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With divergent timelines, why is any one timeline considered "primary"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, this article hits enough sweet spots of mine (plausible-sounding ,if "boring" anomalous science, charming use of body horror (legs walking on their own? six molars and a part of brain alligned as if there was a head there?), causality messing) that the final addendum, good as it is in a fridge horror sense is just a cherry on the delicious cake here.
Enthusiastic upvote, please don't change a thing x3.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am in agreement. I like the article, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part where they twitch a bunch because of nearby Schroedinger Errors really, really makes this one remind me of Bioshock Infinite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This might be one of the few cases where telling rather than showing could be better suited. I mean, you're dealing with something that very few people really understand. I only get it because I'm into alternatehistory and the concept of parallel and otherwise timelines is second nature.

Hell, I don't see many people outside of the Foundation's specialists on the thing really grasping the magnitude of what's happening, without it being spelled out. It's hard to fathom, on your own, the idea that of all the timelines we are aware of, the only one making it past this (subjective) point is us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Advice? How would you get to that execution?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may pull it and revise. The author here is exactly right about the point of the article, but my problem here is the amount of exposition necessary to get to that point:

There is something <i>very specific</i> going on here: for some reason, only a very particular timeline has continued viability. But we aren't sure what's going on in the timelines we can't talk to for fear of contamination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that's true, then I like the concept, but I dislike the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a handful(well, infinite, but you get what I mean) of timelines *juuuust* ever so slightly out of sync with our own. The changes are likely to be as miniscule as one or two differently-charged particles. But these changes butterfly, and eventually, people who are(by chance) more closely entangled to their "whole self"(all versions of you from the multiverse) are phasing in and out of *our* timeline. These are noticable because these people suffered horrific accidents that they did not in our world, or they *didn't* suffer these accidents in our world and the alter-thems did in another. Regardless, they're coming through. Not wholy, just enough to notice. Glitching through.

So, the Multiverse Foundation of Foundations(patent pending) was founded and everyone sort of agreed on a similar way to track known problem-people using rare radioactive metals. So when those show up in quantities improbable to happen naturally, we know there's a breach.

Then, one day, 41 *million* people started showing those signs. And then 21% of the entire human population got (nearly unnoticeable, harmless) radiation poisoning. The Alter-foundations were telling us that something *BAD* happened, and somehow, we're the only ones(in contact with the others, that is) that didn't have that happen.

And we don't know *why* it missed us, or if the ones we got the message from were actually slightly 'ahead' of us in time, and if we just haven't gotten to that 'point' yet.

I think that explains it.

Do I get a medal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it very obtuse and difficult to find the point. Actually, I'm still not sure what's the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This relies too heavily on pseudo-jargon up front, so that by the time the actual point of this article arrives at the very end, I'm not really interested.

This is positively rated, so it's working some level. Vox populi, vox Dei. For me personally though, and I suspect a few others, this is conceptually akin to reading the phone book out loud to someone for ten minutes, then going "and the call was coming from inside the house."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant to deeply underplay it. Maybe that didn't work? I'd gotten sign-off from the chat, but — yes. There's definitely some dryness. But there's nowhere for a log to go, because it's not really the SCP itself that's the punchline: the SCP lets us detect the punchline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the fridge horror you're talking about the implication that the Foundation's fucked up primary universe is the best of all possible timelines? If so, it doesn't pay off well. The buildup is awkward and obfuscated. Overall, the piece is underwhelming for the implied magnitude.

Is this article derived from this?

Dr. ██████: Do you know what it means for me to say that to you? That it is all for the best? That this is the best of all possible worlds? That this is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think "this is boring" downvotes are the price I'm just going to have to pay for banking so heavily on fridge horror. Better to lose those points than to lose the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't doing anything for me. Neutral vote.

Edit: On further consideration this was boring enough that it bothered me. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed that just now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. There's an SCP-XXX in the last addendum, but other than that little slip, very nice work.
